summary: This guest post presents a nuanced dialogue between two parents (one a former English teacher, one a math professional) about PARCC standardized testing and Common Core education reforms. While both support having common educational standards and see value in consistent assessment, they strongly oppose using test scores for teacher evaluations, citing statistical unreliability of value-added models. They discuss concerns about computer-based testing widening achievement gaps and reducing computers to 'paperweights' instead of learning tools. The authors conclude by letting their children take PARCC while advocating for systemic change from within, noting that New Jersey has stepped back from high-stakes implementation compared to New York.
key_points: Common standards for curriculum can be valuable, but standardized testing should be separated from teacher evaluation—the models simply aren't accurate enough at the individual level
Computer-based testing represents a massive missed opportunity: technology should be a teaching tool for experiments and visualization, not primarily a testing medium
The achievement gap widens when computer-based tests essentially measure keyboard proficiency and technology access rather than actual knowledge
Schools treating PARCC as high-stakes end up doing harmful test prep at the expense of real learning—ironically losing the most
Whether to opt out depends on local implementation: low-stakes environments in NJ versus Cuomo's push for 50% weight on test scores in NY create very different calculations for parents
money_quotes: Having student test results impact teacher evaluations is my biggest objection to PARCC, by far.
Here's the most amazing tool human beings have ever invented, and we're going to use it primarily as a paperweight.
Students cannot be taught to think critically in six months.
I also found NCLB naïve in its 'every child can learn it all' attitude. I mean, yes, sure, any child can learn. But kids aren't starting out at the same place or with the same support.
Ironically, schools that treat the PARCC like a high-stakes test are the ones losing the most.
Shame on us, as grown ups, for polluting our kids' education with our anxieties!
The models we have today, even the most state-of-the-art ones, can give you useful aggregate information, but they just don't work at that level of detail.
key_insight: Standardized testing and common educational standards are fundamentally different issues that get unhelpfully lumped together, and while consistent standards can benefit students, the statistical models used for teacher evaluation are too unreliable to be fair or effective.
